title: Rotary Drilling Fundamentals
content: Rotary drilling is the most common method used to drill oil and gas wells. The process involves rotating a drill bit at the bottom of the hole while drilling fluid (mud) is pumped down through the drill string to cool the bit and carry cuttings to the surface. The rotary table or top drive provides the rotational force. Typical penetration rates range from 10 to 50 feet per hour depending on formation hardness. The weight on bit (WOB) is controlled by the number of drill collars in the bottom hole assembly. A standard BHA includes drill collars, stabilizers, and the drill bit.

title: Drilling Fluid Properties and Management
content: Drilling fluid, commonly called drilling mud, serves multiple critical functions: cooling and lubricating the drill bit, carrying cuttings to the surface, maintaining hydrostatic pressure to prevent formation fluids from entering the wellbore, and suspending cuttings when circulation stops. Key properties include density (measured in ppg or sg), viscosity (measured with Marsh funnel), filter loss (API and HTHP), pH level (typically 8.5-11.0), and sand content. Water-based muds (WBM) are most common, with typical densities from 8.5 to 18 ppg. Oil-based muds (OBM) are used for challenging wells with high temperature or reactive shales.

title: Wellbore Stability and Hole Cleaning
content: Wellbore stability is critical for successful drilling operations. Instability manifests as borehole breakouts, washouts, or tight holes. The mud weight window is defined by the collapse pressure on the lower bound and the fracture gradient on the upper bound. Tripping pipe must be done at appropriate speeds to avoid surge and swab pressures. Hole cleaning efficiency depends on flow rate, pipe rotation, and mud rheology. Minimum annular velocity for adequate hole cleaning in deviated wells is typically 100-150 ft/min. In horizontal wells, cuttings beds can form on the low side if flow rate is insufficient.

title: Directional Drilling and Well Planning
content: Directional drilling involves controlling the wellbore trajectory to reach targets that are not directly below the surface location. Key terms include build rate (measured in degrees per 100 feet), dogleg severity, inclination (angle from vertical), and azimuth (compass direction). Measurement while drilling (MWD) tools provide real-time survey data. Logging while drilling (LWD) adds formation evaluation capability. Rotary steerable systems (RSS) allow continuous steering while rotating the entire drill string. Build rates typically range from 2 to 8 degrees per 100 feet.

title: Casing Design and Cementing
content: Casing strings are installed to maintain wellbore integrity. A typical well has surface casing (20-36 inches), intermediate casing (13-3/8 to 16 inches), and production casing (5-1/2 to 9-5/8 inches). Casing is sized based on well depth, formation pressures, and production requirements. API 5CT specifies casing grades from H40 to Q125 with yield strengths from 40,000 to 125,000 psi. Cementing involves pumping cement slurry into the annulus between casing and wellbore. Primary cement jobs must achieve adequate top of cement (TOC) and zonal isolation.

title: Blowout Prevention and Well Control
content: Well control is the management of formation pressures to prevent unplanned release of fluids (kicks). A kick occurs when formation pressure exceeds hydrostatic pressure of the mud column. Blowout preventers (BOPs) are installed at the wellhead to provide mechanical barriers. A typical BOP stack includes annular preventers and pipe rams capable of closing around tubulars. Kick detection methods include monitoring pit volumes, flow rates, and hookload. Maximum allowable surface pressure (MASP) is calculated based on the weakest exposed formation.

title: Drill Bit Selection and Optimization
content: Drill bit selection is based on formation type, depth, and drilling objectives. Tricone roller cone bits are used for hard formations with IADC codes from 1-1-1 to 8-3-7. Polycrystalline diamond compact (PDC) bits are preferred for soft to medium formations with higher rates of penetration. Mechanical specific energy (MSE) calculations identify inefficient drilling. Optimal WOB for PDC bits typically ranges from 2 to 6 klbs per inch of bit diameter. Bit records should track ROP, WOB, RPM, torque, and hydraulic parameters.

title: Well Abandonment Procedures
content: Well abandonment (plugging and abandonment, PA) requires proper isolation of all hydrocarbon zones. Regulatory agencies require cement plugs at specific intervals to prevent fluid migration between zones. Common plug locations include below the deepest hydrocarbon zone, at each productive interval, above the shallowest hydrocarbon zone, and at surface. Bridge plugs or cement plugs are set to provide mechanical barriers. Casing may be cut and pulled above surface or below a specified depth.

title: Personal Protective Equipment Requirements
content: All personnel on oil and gas facilities must wear appropriate PPE at all times. Mandatory PPE includes hard hat, safety glasses, steel-toed boots, hearing protection in designated areas, and fire-resistant clothing (FRC) when exposed to hydrocarbons. PPE must meet ANSI/ISEA standards. Hard hats must be replaced after any impact or every 5 years. Safety glasses must have ANSI Z87.1 rating. Boots must have ASTM F2413 impact and compression resistance.

title: Hot Work Permits and Fire Prevention
content: Hot work includes welding, cutting, grinding, and any operation producing sparks or flames. A hot work permit is required before commencing any hot work on or near hydrocarbon processing areas. The permit must be issued by the site safety authority after gas testing confirms the atmosphere is within safe limits (less than 10% of LEL). Fire watch personnel must be posted during and 30 minutes after hot work completion. A minimum 35-foot exclusion zone must be established around hot work areas.

title: Confined Space Entry Procedures
content: Confined spaces on oil and gas facilities include vessels, tanks, pits, pipelines, and manholes. Entry requires a permit signed by the entry supervisor. Before entry, atmospheric testing must confirm oxygen levels between 19.5% and 23.5%, flammable gases below 10% LEL, and toxic gases below permissible exposure limits. Ventilation must be maintained during entry. An attendant must be posted at the entrance at all times.

title: Hazardous Area Classification and Electrical Safety
content: Hazardous areas are classified according to API 500 and IEC 60079 based on the likelihood of flammable gases being present. Class I, Division 1 areas have ignitable concentrations under normal operation. Division 2 areas have ignitable concentrations only under abnormal conditions. Zone 0, 1, and 2 classifications follow IEC standards. Electrical equipment in classified areas must be rated for the specific hazard class and group.

title: Process Safety Management
content: Process Safety Management (PSM) under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 applies to processes involving highly hazardous chemicals above threshold quantities. The 14 elements of PSM include process information, hazard analysis, operating procedures, training, mechanical integrity, management of change, pre-startup safety review, hot work permits, incident investigation, emergency planning, compliance audits, trade secrets, employee participation, and contractor safety.

title: Fall Protection in Oil and Gas Operations
content: Fall protection is required whenever personnel work at heights above 6 feet (OSHA requirement). On drilling rigs and production platforms, fall protection includes guardrails, personal fall arrest systems (PFAS), safety nets, and restraint systems. Full body harnesses must comply with ANSI Z359.11 and be inspected before each use. Anchor points must support 5,000 pounds or be designed with a 2:1 safety factor.

title: Centrifugal Pump Selection and Sizing
content: Centrifugal pumps are the most common type used in oil and gas operations. Pump selection depends on flow rate, total dynamic head (TDH), fluid properties, and system requirements. The affinity laws relate speed, flow, head, and power: Q1/Q2 = N1/N2, H1/H2 = (N1/N2)^2, P1/P2 = (N1/N2)^3. Net positive suction head required (NPSHr) must be less than NPSHa to avoid cavitation. Specific speed determines the best impeller type: radial (Ns < 1500), mixed flow (1500 < Ns < 4000), or axial (Ns > 4000).

title: Heat Exchanger Design and Maintenance
content: Shell and tube heat exchangers are the most common type in oil and gas processing. Design follows TEMA standards (R, C, or B service). The overall heat transfer coefficient (U) depends on fluid properties, flow geometry, and fouling factors. LMTD method calculates heat duty: Q = U * A * F * LMTD. Fouling reduces heat transfer efficiency over time. Baffle spacing typically ranges from 20% to 100% of shell diameter.

title: Compressor Operations and Maintenance
content: Reciprocating and centrifugal compressors are used for gas compression in production and pipeline operations. Reciprocating compressors handle higher pressure ratios (up to 4:1 per stage) with lower flow rates. Centrifugal compressors provide high flow rates at moderate pressure ratios (1.5:1 to 3:1 per stage). Polytropic efficiency typically ranges from 75% to 85%. Surge is a critical operating limit for centrifugal compressors - anti-surge control systems prevent operation in the surge region.

title: Pressure Vessel Design and Inspection
content: Pressure vessels are designed according to ASME BPVC Section VIII. The minimum wall thickness for a cylindrical shell under internal pressure is t = PR/(SE - 0.6P) where P is design pressure, R is inside radius, S is allowable stress, and E is joint efficiency. Inspection intervals follow API 510: external inspection annually, internal inspection every 5-10 years based on corrosion rate. Hydrostatic testing at 1.5x MAWP is required for new vessels.

title: Electric Submersible Pump Systems
content: Electric submersible pumps (ESPs) are used for artificial lift in high-volume wells. A complete ESP system includes downhole motor, seal/protector, gas separator (optional), pump intake, multi-stage centrifugal pump, power cable, surface transformer, and variable speed drive (VSD). Pump stages are selected based on required total dynamic head and flow rate. Typical ESP installations produce 200 to 80,000 bbl/day. Run life averages 2-5 years depending on operating conditions.

title: Rod Pumping Systems
content: Sucker rod pumping is the most common artificial lift method worldwide. The system consists of a surface pumping unit (beam pump), sucker rod string, and downhole pump (plunger and barrel). API RP 11L provides design methods for rod pump installations. Key design parameters include stroke length, strokes per minute, rod size, and pump depth. Polished rod load analysis uses the wave equation to determine downhole card shape.

title: Pipeline Design and Integrity Management
content: Oil and gas pipelines are designed according to API 5L for line pipe and ASME B31.4 (liquid) or B31.8 (gas). Wall thickness is calculated as t = PD/(2SE*F*T) where P is design pressure, D is outside diameter, S is specified minimum yield strength (SMYS), E is longitudinal joint factor, F is design factor (0.72 for class 1, 0.60 for class 2-4). External corrosion protection uses cathodic protection (CP) with impressed current or sacrificial anodes.

title: Primary Recovery Mechanisms
content: Primary recovery utilizes natural reservoir energy to produce hydrocarbons. The main mechanisms are solution gas drive, gas cap drive, water drive, gravity drainage, and combination drive. Solution gas drive reservoirs produce by gas liberation from oil as pressure drops below bubble point. Recovery factors typically range from 5% to 25%. Gas cap drive reservoirs benefit from expansion of the gas cap providing pressure support. Water drive reservoirs have natural water influx maintaining pressure. Recovery factors for strong water drive can reach 50-70%.

title: Enhanced Oil Recovery Methods
content: EOR methods are employed after primary and secondary recovery to extract additional hydrocarbons. Thermal methods include steam flooding, cyclic steam stimulation (CSS), and in-situ combustion. Chemical EOR includes polymer flooding (improves sweep efficiency), surfactant flooding (reduces interfacial tension), and alkaline flooding. Miscible gas injection uses CO2 or hydrocarbon gases that miscible with reservoir oil. CO2-EOR can recover an additional 8-15% of original oil in place (OOIP).

title: Well Testing and Performance Analysis
content: Well tests provide critical reservoir information including permeability, skin factor, and reservoir boundaries. Types of well tests include buildup tests, drawdown tests, interference tests, and step-rate tests. Pressure transient analysis uses log-log and semi-log plots to identify flow regimes: wellbore storage, radial flow, and boundary effects. Horner analysis for buildup tests plots pressure vs. Horner time to determine permeability-thickness (kh) and skin.

title: Production Chemistry and Flow Assurance
content: Flow assurance ensures hydrocarbons flow from reservoir to processing facilities without disruption. Key issues include wax deposition, asphaltene precipitation, scale formation, gas hydrates, and emulsion formation. Wax (paraffin) deposits when temperature drops below the cloud point (typically 40-70 degrees C for waxy crudes). Asphaltenes precipitate due to pressure changes or incompatible fluids. Scale types include calcium carbonate (CaCO3), barium sulfate (BaSO4), and iron sulfide (FeS).

title: Artificial Lift Selection and Optimization
content: Artificial lift is required when reservoir pressure is insufficient to produce at economic rates. Selection criteria include production rate, well depth, fluid properties, and operating environment. Rod pumping (beam units) is suitable for rates up to 1,500 bbl/day and depths to 10,000 ft. ESP systems handle high rates (200-80,000 bbl/day) at depths up to 15,000 ft. Gas lift uses injected gas to reduce fluid column weight and is flexible for varying production rates.

title: Gas Processing and Treatment
content: Raw natural gas requires processing to meet pipeline specifications. Key processes include dehydration (removing water vapor to less than 7 lb/MMSCF), sweetening (removing H2S and CO2), NGL extraction (recovering natural gas liquids), and sulfur recovery. Glycol dehydration uses triethylene glycol (TEG) with typical dew point depressions of 50-100 degrees F. The Claus process converts H2S to elemental sulfur with recovery rates exceeding 99%.

title: Reservoir Characterization and Petrophysics
content: Reservoir characterization integrates geological, geophysical, and petrophysical data to describe reservoir properties. Key parameters include porosity (typically 5-30%), permeability (0.1-10,000 mD), water saturation (Sw), and net-to-gross ratio. Water saturation is calculated from resistivity logs using Archie equation: Sw = (a * Rw / (phi^m * Rt))^(1/n). Special core analysis provides relative permeability, capillary pressure, and wettability data for reservoir simulation.

title: Workover and Well Intervention Operations
content: Workover operations restore or enhance well productivity. Common workover reasons include mechanical failure, formation damage, water shutoff, and recompletion. Coiled tubing (CT) units provide rigless intervention capability for cleanouts, logging, and treatments. Wireline operations include slickline (mechanical operations) and electric line (logging and perforating). Stimulation treatments include hydraulic fracturing and acidizing.

title: Reservoir Engineering Fundamentals
content: Reservoir engineering applies scientific principles to optimize hydrocarbon recovery. Material balance equations (MBE) relate production to reservoir pressure decline and fluid expansion. Darcy law governs fluid flow through porous media: q = -kA(mu)(dP/dx). Pseudo-steady state flow occurs during boundary-dominated production. Inflow performance relationship (IPR) curves show the relationship between flowing bottomhole pressure and production rate. Vogel IPR equation for solution gas drive reservoirs is q/qmax = 1 - 0.2(Pwf/Pr) - 0.8(Pwf/Pr)^2.

title: Multiphase Flow in Pipes
content: Multiphase flow in production tubing and surface pipelines involves complex interactions between gas, oil, and water phases. Flow regimes include bubble, slug, churn, and annular flow. Pressure gradient calculations account for friction, gravity, and acceleration components. The Beggs and Brill correlation is widely used for multiphase pressure drop calculations. Holdup refers to the fraction of pipe cross-section occupied by the liquid phase.

title: Stimulation Design and Optimization
content: Hydraulic fracturing creates conductive pathways from the wellbore into the reservoir. Treatment design specifies pump rate, fluid volume, proppant concentration, and proppant type. Typical pump rates range from 20 to 100 bbl/min. Proppant sizes range from 20/40 to 100 mesh sand or ceramic. Fracture half-length targets range from 200 to 2,000 ft. Matrix acidizing in sandstones uses 12-15% HCl with 3% HF (mud acid) to dissolve formation damage.

title: Formation Damage Mechanisms
content: Formation damage reduces well productivity by impairing near-wellbore permeability. Damage mechanisms include: fines migration (movement of fine particles blocking pore throats), clay swelling (water sensitivity), emulsion blockage, asphaltene deposition, scale formation, and bacterial growth. The skin factor quantifies formation damage. Skin values greater than 0 indicate damage, while negative skin indicates stimulation.

title: Enhanced Oil Recovery Screening
content: EOR screening criteria help identify suitable methods for specific reservoirs. Thermal methods (steam, in-situ combustion) are best for heavy oils (greater than 10 degrees API) with depths less than 3,000 ft and thickness greater than 20 ft. Chemical methods (polymer, surfactant, ASP) suit light to medium oils (20-35 degrees API) with permeability greater than 50 mD. CO2 miscible flooding works for oils with minimum miscibility pressure (MMP) less than fracture pressure.

title: Water Treatment and Disposal in Oil Operations
content: Produced water is the largest waste stream in oil and gas operations. Treatment methods include gravity separation, chemical treatment (emulsion breakers, coagulants), filtration (sand, cartridge, membrane), and flotation (induced or dissolved gas). Disposal options include reinjection (disposal or waterflood), surface discharge (after meeting environmental standards), and evaporation ponds. Oil in produced water must typically be reduced to less than 15-30 mg/L before disposal. Heavy metals, naturally occurring radioactive materials (NORM), and dissolved solids must also be managed.

title: Well Integrity Management
content: Well integrity is the application of technical, operational, and organizational solutions to reduce the risk of uncontrolled release of formation fluids throughout the life cycle of a well. Well barriers are verified through pressure testing, cement evaluation logs, and corrosion monitoring. The life cycle approach includes design, construction, operation, and abandonment phases. API RP 90 and NORSOK D-010 provide frameworks for well integrity management. Annulus pressure monitoring is essential for early detection of barrier failures.

title: Mud Logging and Formation Evaluation
content: Mud logging provides real-time information about drilling operations and formation characteristics. Key measurements include gas analysis (total gas, chromatograph), drilling parameters (ROP, WOB, torque, pump pressure), and cutting description (lithology, fossil content, hydrocarbon shows). The gas chromatograph measures C1 through C5 hydrocarbons. Ratio analysis (C1/C2, C3/C4) helps distinguish between gas zones, oil zones, and wet gas. D-exponent and corrected d-exponent normalize ROP for changes in WOB and RPM.

title: Tubing and Casing Corrosion Management
content: Corrosion in oil and gas wells is caused by CO2, H2S, high temperature, and high salinity brines. CO2 corrosion produces iron carbonate (FeCO3) scale. H2S causes sulfide stress cracking (SSC) and hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC). Corrosion rates are managed through material selection (corrosion-resistant alloys, CRA), chemical inhibition (film formers, oxygen scavengers), cathodic protection, and internal coatings. Monitoring methods include corrosion coupons, electrical resistance probes, and ultrasonic thickness measurements.

title: Offshore Platform Design and Operations
content: Offshore platforms are designed based on water depth, environmental conditions, and production requirements. Types include fixed platforms (steel or concrete), compliant towers, tension leg platforms (TLPs), semi-submersibles, and floating production systems (FPS). Design criteria follow API RP 2A for fixed platforms. Environmental loading includes wind, wave, current, and seismic forces. Design wave heights range from 30-100 feet depending on location. Mooring systems for floating platforms include catenary and taut-leg designs.

title: Hydraulic Fracturing Operations
content: Hydraulic fracturing (fracking) is a stimulation technique that creates fractures in rock formations to increase the flow of hydrocarbons. The process involves pumping fracturing fluid at high pressure to break down the formation, then placing proppant (sand or ceramic beads) to keep the fractures open. Typical fracturing fluids include slickwater (water + friction reducer), crosslinked gel, and linear gel. Pump rates of 60-100 bbl/min are common. Total fluid volumes range from 500,000 to several million gallons per well.

title: Natural Gas Hydrates and Prevention
content: Gas hydrates (clathrates) are ice-like crystalline structures formed when water molecules create a cage around small gas molecules (primarily methane, ethane, propane) at high pressure and low temperature. Hydrate formation conditions: temperatures below 25 degrees C and pressures above 500 psia for methane. Prevention methods include thermodynamic inhibitors (methanol, ethylene glycol at 10-80 vol%), low-dosage hydrate inhibitors (LDHI), and thermal insulation of pipelines. Dehydration to remove water is the most reliable prevention method.

title: Production Optimization Through Nodal Analysis
content: Nodal analysis divides the production system at a single point (the node) to analyze the relationship between the reservoir and the surface. The two major components are the inflow performance relationship (IPR) and the vertical lift performance (VLP). The intersection of IPR and VLP curves determines the operating point (flowing bottomhole pressure and production rate). VLP curves account for multiphase flow effects in the tubing. Changing tubing diameter, choke size, or artificial lift parameters shifts the operating point.

title: Gas Lift Design and Operations
content: Gas lift is an artificial lift method that injects gas into the production tubing to reduce the fluid column density. Continuous gas lift injects gas through side-pocket mandrels with gas lift valves. Key design parameters include injection depth, injection rate, and operating pressure. Gas lift valves are typically pressure-operated (balance dome or spring-loaded). Kick-off valves initiate the unloading process, while the operating valve controls steady-state injection. Optimal gas injection rate balances the cost of gas compression against the incremental oil production.

title: Separator Design and Operation
content: Production separators separate well fluids into oil, gas, and water streams. Types include two-phase (gas-liquid) and three-phase (oil-gas-water) separators. Design parameters include operating pressure, temperature, retention time, and droplet size requirements. Horizontal separators handle higher gas-to-oil ratios (GOR). Vertical separators are preferred for high liquid loads and solids-laden fluids. Mist eliminators (vane packs, wire mesh, coalescing) remove liquid droplets from the gas stream. Typical retention times: 30-60 seconds for gas, 1-3 minutes for liquid.

title: Refinery Distillation and Crude Processing
content: Atmospheric distillation is the primary separation process in refineries. Crude oil is heated to 350-400 degrees C in the furnace and fed to the distillation column. Fractions separate based on boiling point ranges: light gases (C1-C4, less than 30 degrees C), naphtha (30-200 degrees C), kerosene (200-250 degrees C), diesel (250-350 degrees C), and atmospheric residue (greater than 350 degrees C). Vacuum distillation further separates the residue at 25-40 mmHg to avoid thermal cracking.

title: Seismic Data Acquisition and Processing
content: Seismic surveys use acoustic waves to image subsurface geology. 2D seismic provides a cross-sectional view along a single line. 3D seismic provides volumetric coverage with bin sizes typically 12.5 x 12.5 meters. 4D seismic (time-lapse) monitors changes in the reservoir over time. Acquisition involves a source (vibroseis or dynamite on land, air guns offshore) and receivers (geophones or hydrophones). Processing steps include velocity analysis, normal moveout (NMO) correction, stacking, and migration.

title: Environmental Compliance in Oil and Gas
content: Oil and gas operations are subject to environmental regulations including air emissions (EPA Clean Air Act), water discharge (Clean Water Act), waste management (RCRA), and soil contamination (CERCLA). Methane emission reduction programs include LDAR (Leak Detection and Repair) surveys using optical gas imaging cameras. Flaring restrictions require gas capture or use. Produced water discharge must meet NPDES permit requirements. Environmental impact assessments (EIA) are required before new development.

title: Subsea Production Systems
content: Subsea production systems are used for deepwater developments (water depths greater than 1,000 ft). Components include subsea trees (wet or dry), manifolds, flowlines, umbilicals, and risers. Subsea processing includes boosting (subsea pumps), separation, and reinjection. Control systems use electro-hydraulic multiplexed control with response times optimized for the water depth.脐带缆 (umbilicals) carry hydraulic fluid, electrical power, and fiber optic signals from the surface facility to subsea equipment.

title: Natural Gas Liquids Recovery
content: Natural gas liquids (NGLs) include ethane, propane, butane, isobutane, and natural gasoline. Recovery methods include lean oil absorption, refrigeration, and turboexpander processes. Turboexpander plants achieve NGL recoveries of 90-99% by rapidly expanding gas to cool it below its dew point. De-methanizer columns separate methane from NGLs at pressures of 300-500 psig. Product specifications require NGL mixtures to meet vapor pressure limits for transportation and storage.

title: Well Perforating and Completions
content: Perforating creates flow paths between the wellbore and the reservoir. Methods include bullet perforating (less common), shaped charge (jet) perforating, and abrasive jetting. API RP 43 defines testing procedures for shaped charges. Key perforating parameters include penetration depth (typically 6-48 inches), entry hole diameter (0.3-0.5 inches), shot density (1-12 shots per foot), and phasing (0, 60, 90, 120, or 180 degrees). Underbalanced perforating prevents formation damage by allowing immediate flow-back after perforating.

title: Real-Time Drilling Monitoring and Automation
content: Real-time drilling monitoring uses surface and downhole sensors to optimize operations and detect problems early. Surface sensors measure hookload, torque, standpipe pressure, pit volume, and flow rate. Downhole MWD/LWD tools provide directional surveys, gamma ray, resistivity, and pressure data. Advanced systems use machine learning algorithms to detect kicks, lost circulation, and pipe connection gas. Automated drilling systems can control WOB, RPM, and flow rate in real-time to optimize ROP.

title: Carbon Capture and Storage in Oil Operations
content: Carbon capture and storage (CCS) involves capturing CO2 from industrial sources and injecting it underground for permanent storage. In EOR applications, CO2 is injected to improve oil recovery while being stored in the reservoir. Capture technologies include post-combustion (amine scrubbing), pre-combustion (gasification), and oxy-fuel combustion. Geological storage sites include depleted oil and gas reservoirs, deep saline aquifers, and unmineable coal seams. Monitoring includes pressure monitoring, seismic surveys, and groundwater sampling.

title: LNG Production and Regasification
content: Liquefied natural gas (LNG) is produced by cooling natural gas to approximately -162 degrees C (260 degrees F), reducing its volume by 600 times. The liquefaction process involves pre-treatment (removal of water, CO2, and heavy hydrocarbons), refrigeration (cascade, mixed refrigerant, or expander cycles), and storage. LNG carriers maintain cargo temperatures at -162 degrees C using insulated membrane tanks. Regasification terminals use submerged combustion vaporizers or open rack vaporizers to convert LNG back to gas for pipeline delivery.

title: Drilling Waste Management
content: Drilling waste management addresses cuttings, used drilling fluids, and other materials generated during drilling operations. Treatment methods include thermal desorption (removing hydrocarbons from cuttings), bioremediation (microbial degradation of hydrocarbons), and solidification/stabilization. Cuttings reinjection (CRI) disposes of drilling waste by injecting slurries into permitted subsurface formations. Waste minimization strategies include fluid recycling, solids control optimization, and water-based mud substitution for oil-based muds where possible.

title: Oil Spill Response and Containment
content: Oil spill response follows the ICS (Incident Command System) framework. Primary containment methods include mechanical booms (containment, absorbent, fire), skimmers (weir, oleophilic, vacuum), and in-situ burning. Chemical dispersants break oil into small droplets to enhance natural biodegradation. Bioremediation uses nutrients to stimulate indigenous microorganisms. Shoreline cleanup techniques include manual removal, pressure washing, and sediment relocation. Response time is critical - 70-80% of spilled oil can evaporate within the first 24 hours.

title: Metering and Fiscal Measurement in Production
content: Fiscal metering measures produced hydrocarbons for custody transfer and royalty calculations. Oil metering uses positive displacement (PD) meters or turbine meters with density and temperature compensation. Gas metering uses orifice meters (AGA Report No. 3), ultrasonic meters, or Coriolis meters. Custody transfer accuracy requirements are typically +/-0.25% for oil and +/-1.0% for gas. Meter proving (calibration) is performed regularly using pipeline provers or master meters.

title: Produced Water Reinjection
content: Produced water reinjection (PWRI) disposes of produced water by injecting it into subsurface formations. Water quality requirements vary by injection target: for disposal wells, suspended solids less than 5-50 mg/L and oil content less than 15-30 mg/L. Injection pressure must remain below formation fracture pressure for voidage replacement. Water compatibility testing prevents scale formation and formation damage. The water injection system includes filtration (media, cartridge, or membrane), chemical treatment (oxygen scavenger, scale inhibitor, biocide), and high-pressure pumps.

title: Vibration Analysis for Rotating Equipment
content: Vibration analysis is the primary predictive maintenance tool for rotating equipment (pumps, compressors, turbines). Acceptable vibration levels follow API 610 (pumps) and API 617 (compressors) standards. Vibration frequency analysis identifies specific faults: unbalance (1x RPM), misalignment (1x and 2x RPM), bearing defects (BPFO, BPFI frequencies), and blade pass frequency. Overall vibration velocity in mm/s RMS is a common health metric. ISO 10816 provides vibration severity classifications for industrial machines.

title: Flare System Design and Safety
content: Flare systems provide safe disposal of excess hydrocarbons during normal operations, startup, shutdown, and emergency conditions. Design follows API 521 for pressure relieving and depressurizing. Key parameters include flare radiation limits (1,500-3,000 Btu/hr/ft2 for personnel, 2,000-5,000 for equipment), noise limits, and visible flame height. Flare types include elevated flares (single-point, multi-point, combined) and ground flares. Flame detection systems (UV/IR sensors) must activate within 3 seconds. Steam or air assist reduces smoke formation.

title: Chemical Injection Systems in Production
content: Chemical injection systems deliver treatment chemicals to production wells, pipelines, and facilities. Common chemicals include corrosion inhibitors (filming amines), scale inhibitors (phosphonates, polymers), demulsifiers, paraffin inhibitors, biocides (glutaraldehyde, THPS), and oxygen scavengers (sodium bisulfite, catalyzed sodium erythorbate). Injection methods include continuous injection, batch treatment, and squeeze treatments. Metering pumps (diaphragm, plunger) deliver precise chemical volumes. Chemical consumption tracking optimizes treatment programs and reduces costs.
